sentences.text: When I say I didn't see my father anymore-- but actually, we seen him until after Yom Kippur.
My mother, we never saw her anymore since the day we arrive in Auschwitz.
She was killed the night we all arrive together.
We told the other people Rhodes Island, which there were 1,752 people.
And out of them, we came back alive 150. 149 to be exact.
My father, we saw him until October, after Yom Kippur.
He was a little bit nice-looking and plumpy.
The Germans mistook him for a young person.
And he was amongst the youngsters.
And he was working after the 40 days-- he was working in the mine of coal, coal mine.
And friends of mine that came back, they told me the story.
In the times he was working in those mines, he was not sick.
But they were assigning people for night shift and day shift.
My father was assigned for night shift that week.
And the-- the block where he was living, they find that there was a person sick with typhoid.
So during the day, the Germans put fire to the whole block because one person was sick with typhoid, and they feared that-- they feared that the whole block was going to be infected.
So it was all-- they seal it and they put, you know, fire on it.
My father, he didn't knew a thing.
He was on the night shift.
In the morning, you know, he arrived from his shift and he wanted to go to his place, to his block.
And they find him, you know, sealed nearly to be-- to be, you know burned.
And say, what are you doing here?
They ask him.
Say, well, I live here.
I am in the night shift.
Oh, you live here?
Say, go in.
And they go-- tey go-- you know, he went in, and they burned him alive-- because he was not even sick.
He was-- was not even typhoid or nothing.
And they burned him.
Because another friend of mine, which is a survival and lives in Zimbabwe, they told me about-- he told me about the story.
He's a survivor from these coal mines.
So we were left, you know, the two brothers, which I never saw them again.
But they were sent to Germany to work.
Another friend of mine, she was to this same-- another lady, she was sent to those German camps.
She related me-- she's also a survivor.
So, eventually later on, about November or December-- she doesn't remember exactly the time-- she saw my one brother.
He was being carried away in a-- in a-- a chart.
He was half-dead, half-alive.
But when you were not well, they were carrying you like that to put, you know, in the crematorium.
She saw my brother because she knew him very well, you know, before the war.
Because my brothers, the two of them, they were teachers in all these languages.
English, Math, whatever.
And this girl, she was-- she was a pupil of his.
And then I saw, he said, my teacher going in a chart.
He was still alive, because he was moving his legs and his arms.
And she saw him alive, but she knew he was going to get-- you know, they were carrying him to the crematorium.
But he was not even dead when they put him there.
My other brothers-- I-- my other brother, I don't know what happened to him.
But the one brother, the lady told me the story.
And another guy-- a guy-- told me my father end.
But my little sister, she was still with us at the time.
And I was just saying it was just the last-- the last selection.
And my little sister was amongst the last one because they wanted, at that same time, you know, so many to be, you know, sent to the crematorium.
When, you know, they reach the figure that they wanted, they stop.
The next one in line, it was our turn.
Which-- the next day, we were also selected to be killed.
So once you are selected to be killed, they didn't even bother to give you any food.
No soup, no bread, no nothing.
So that night we spent in the-- in the-- like they say, in the waiting room of the death.
We were there.
I saw the blocks.
They were horrible to look, to sleep, and everything.
But what we saw in that-- in that horror room is something unbelievable.
There were, you know, I don't think so-- nobody has ever seen that-- nobody, survivor-- has seen that room because-- or you were a survivor and you never reached that stage.
Or you were, you know, you went there and you went to the crematorium.
So you cannot-- you cannot tell the world what it was like.
In any case, it was a big hole.
Inside of the walls, there were no planks or whatever.
Inside of the walls, there were like holes, like caves inside of the walls.
There were big caves.
And in those caves, you know they were like planks.
But what it was different than all the other-- the other blocks, because it was infested with rats.
Big rats like cats.
So now, prior to this day that we went in this place, you know, we were given our food.
I say that always keep, you know, our ration for the next day.
My little sister was already-- was already, you know, selected.
She was not with us anymore.
But I remained with my other sister and other people, the other girls.
So they-- they-- they told us to go in those-- in those places to go to sleep.
I had it it in a piece of material that I found somewhere, my bread from the previous day that I was keeping for-- you know, my one ration.
The other one I already eaten for the next day.
With that piece of bologna that it was made of the human flesh, whatever.
And we were there, you know, waiting to be-- and then we couldn't dare eat it because it was for the next day food, though we were starving.
And then we went to sleep.
While I was sleeping, a big rat come on top of me.
And it was pulling at my-- at my parcel.
It wants to eat the bread and the bologna.
And then I was saying to my sister, Diamante, the rat wants the food.
What shall I do?
And then she say, keep it.
You must not give it to him, to the rat, because this is our food for tomorrow.
And I was holding, you know.
And the rat, it comes two rats.
One on each side pulling at my foot-- but I was not even afraid of the rats, big rats.
And they were not mouses.
They were big rats.
And they were pulling and pulling.
At the end, I was fed up.
I say, all right, you keep the food, and then see what happen.
As soon as she took the food, a rat came after it.
She gave it to-- to them because she was scared, what [INAUDIBLE] just to see.
And then, they ate-- we lost the food for the next day.
So in any case, the next day, went without food.
Because once, I told you, you were in that room, they didn't give you-- forgot all about you.
It was finished.
You are already gone through the camp.
200. 200, Because that is the usual-- when we were in that place of Auschwitz where we were, the gas chamber holds 200 people at a time.
So, when it was, you know-- this-- my little sister was, as I told you, selected one selection before us.
She was already the last to go into the crematorium, into the gas chamber.
And she was already inside.
And then by the time they were, you know, evacuating to empty the room, the gas chamber.
You have to empty everything, take away the people, and everything.
And put in another like biscuits, you know, another batch of people.
And it was our turn.
And by the time it comes our turn to be inside, you know, it was already 3 o'clock at night, you know.
All the day we were staying there without food.
And at night, they start killing the other people that they were selected before us.
And then came our turn.
And it was very, very cold.
We had a friend of mine, she was with us.
The only Spanish-speaking people.
All the other ones have already left for Germany.
And we were so cold that she was making jokes at the other girl.
And she say, oh, I wish they will open that oven quicker because we can get hot inside.
[LAUGHS] Because it was something to make fun.
We knew it was the end.
We can't do nothing.
We were resigned to die.
And then, you know, it was not luck-- if it was not God, you ask me what-- at that very moment, there come a jeep in the dark with some Germans.
And the people, you know, start screaming when they saw the jeep, you know, the other ones that speak German, the other 200 people that were with us three.
No.
We were already taken to the open air to go inside of the crematorium.
To the-- to the gas chamber.
It was just like an oven.
What I tell you, we were very cold and we want to go inside of the oven.
It was already in the open.
It was--from the block, they took us into the garden.
Into the garden, there was, you know, this gas chamber.
And then, this jeep arrived.
You know, it was dark.
We heard the noise and whatever it is.
And saw the jeep.
And all the girls that they could speak German, they start screaming throw their voice up.
And then, these Germans came out, say what is the commotion?
Why are you all screaming like that?
We all say, why we shouldn't-- we shouldn't scream?
That is, you know, our end, because now it's-- they opened.
They closed the door.
It was just us.
We say, we're getting killed.
Say, killed?
Who told you that?
We say, well, we know that is the crematorium.
That is the gas chamber.
He say, it is not possible that in the Third Reich, things like that will happen.
Is that because we were taking you to the showers and this and that.
They were trying to speak to them in German.
And they couldn't believe what they were talking about.
That is, it was on the 25th of January, 1945.
And after that, in the history books and the magazine and all these things we heard about, it was the time that the Allied sent an ultimatum to the German people.
According to the-- to the stories of the magazine and the stories in the newspaper, it was only at that time the Allied knew about the crematorium, about the gas chamber, about all the things.
And they say they didn't knew a thing before.
So only at that time, they knew.
And they send, you know, like you say, a warning to the Germans for whoever one Jew you will kill in those chambers, we are going to kill 10 Germans in our possession.
Because at the time, the Germans were already losing the war.
And there were many, many thousands of prisoners in the hands of the Allies.
So now, these people, these Germans, to avoid, you know, their people to be killed, they stopped the killing.
That's why they say it doesn't exist.
And then the girls say, but what about those files going around?
Say, this is just the ovens that they're baking bread for tomorrow.
But it wasn't true.
And then, you know they stop.
That very moment, they stop.
And according to what we heard later on, that very same night they-- they blew away all the crematorium, all the gas chambers and all this, they were in Auschwitz.
People say, I don't know-- what they say is they think that you see now, some people go now after so many years all the time to-- in pilgrimage to see, and they are there.
They are not the original one.
The original one were-- were, you know, were destroyed on 25th, January.
That, you know, is after the war, they reconstructed for the people, for the world to see.
And many also, the-- while we were, you know, working there into the camp sometimes beside that, we could see many, many object of horror, like the guillotine.
We saw the guillotine.
We didn't see actually anything happen.
But while we were passing through, to go for other-- to go to other places, we will see many objects of horror.
And they were trying to kill the Jews in another way.
To take away their nails.
To take away some-- many horror things, you know, utensils.
We saw them in the camp.
And they say they put it-- even to say you can see them.
And many people say also when they go-- I never been back to Auschwitz.
They say there is a place, you know, that they couldn't even disperse all the-- all the ashes of the people.
There are still there ashes because there were millions of people in some place.
It's like soft quicksand.
And you cannot take away all this.
Many ashes were dispersed into the river, but many of them, they are still there in those places.
And then after that night, you know, that-- they say it doesn't exist, the crematorium.
We were taken back to the blocks.
Not block 20, in another block that we never-- never saw during our stay in the camp.
It was a block made of real beds-- you know, full of full beds.
In each bed, there were, you know, satin-- satin, you know, covers.
Maybe they taken from the trains they arrived, I don't know.
But it was a luxurious villa, you know, like where we arrive.
And then the first thing they did, to give us each one box of food by the Red Cross. 5 kilo, or whatever, of food.
Jam, and sardines, and this.
We couldn't believe our eyes what was there.
So we stayed there.
And we thought, you know, that is very nice.
We can stay there-- we can stay there until the end of the war.
It's not so bad because there was no work to be done the next day.
There were no appel.
That lasted, you know, for three days.
Until we were taken out the idea of the-- of the gas chamber and everything.
So after three days, they took us back again to these horrible blocks with the planks and everything.
And we stayed there, you know, until the end.
And that is-- but, you know, there was not any more selections because they were already destroyed.
There were nothing.
But you could still die, and you know, that is 25th of January, but you can still die.
You can still die there, but from disease, from cholera, from other diseases.
But we wouldn't be selected anymore for the crematorium.
Yes, we were.
But every time we hear-- it wasn't bombed.
It was blown up with dynamite, or whatever.
It wasn't bombs.
It was like dynamite.
But it was-- a camp, you will think it's like a camp, a small camp.
The camp was very many kilometers.
If you are in one side of the camp and you went to another side of the camp, you couldn't hear anything.
It was very, very-- you cannot take, for instance, half an hour from one thing to the other camp.
From one side to the next.
It was very, very big.
Imagine even that there were 200, 300 in each block.
Imagine thousands of people living in that area.
There must be many blocks.
And then there were the blocks of the people with skin disease.
Why you call this in English, I don't know.
Like-- [NON-ENGLISH], is a thing-- like in the Bible, one of the 10 plagues that God gives you.
You know, they were that.
And then, they were leaving these people not to kill.
They were experimenting, you know, with the skin and everything.
All sort of things happened in there.
We stay another few weeks in the camp.
But still, with no food.
And, you know, those work [INAUDIBLE].
And then eventually, they put us to work in the [NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH] was a factory there that were making, you know, tubes.
Tubes for the war effort, for the-- for the-- for the guns.
Those tubes in the-- in [NON-ENGLISH], in rubber.
They were doing those things, like a big block thing, to put it in there, like into the guns.
We were working there.
We stayed there a few weeks until now to decide the Allied were advancing.
Auschwitz will be any time occupied by the Russians, because we were liberated by the Russian.
So there was no time to stay anymore in Auschwitz.
The Germans give the, you know, the orders-- give the authority to evacuate Auschwitz.
And then, how can they carry all these people, whatever they want to carry us?
We have to march by walk.
And they call it, you know, the march of the dead, like you say.

That march, you know, is supposed to take us many kilometers away from Auschwitz to a place inside the land-- you know, not the front front with the Russian-- with the-- they're already, you know-- some other prisoner have already dig big, big, you know, holes.
And then we were going to arrive to go there, you know, and to be killed by gun.
And then, the body fell into the thing.
And they were going to kill it that way.
That is the meaning of this march.
And then, they start, you know, taking us to this place.
And we start--
We started the march also at the end of January in 1945.
Because maybe you say three, four days in the camp after that.
At the end of January.
And we start marching.
And we start marching.
We had no strength.
We were thin.
We were sick.
And we were starving.
If you stop, if you stop for a little while, five minutes, the German he was there with a gun.
And then would say to you, [GERMAN]== UP.
He'd say 1, 2, 3.
By three you are not standing up, they will-- they will shoot you on the spot.
My eldest sister was still with me, but not my small sister because she was already killed for the last-- the last Auschwitz thing.
If it was for five more-- maybe for one hour or so.
Because the-- the gas chamber would hold only 200 people.
So if it had held, you know, for instance, 100 people, she would still be alive today and with us.
But it was the last because, when our turn came, she was the last to go.
It was my last thing.
So in any case, that was the last march.
We were saying-- many-- few, if not many, a few of our friends, we find them there.
I don't know where they were before in another camp or Birkenau.
They were in that march.
There were three or four girls.
And then, they were been told 1, 2-- And they were far away in the queue.
And we saw them being shot that night.
Very clearly, I saw a girl.
She was too beautiful.
She was 18.
She was very, very blonde.
And her name was Rosa-- Jeanette Hassen.
Because she's got family in Zimbabwe in South Africa.
I saw her being shot that way, that night because she couldn't-- she couldn't stand up.
So now, we were my sister and myself in that march.
Did you know where you were marching too?
No.
We don't know.
We were just walking.
Walking in the night and the day.
It was the night.
It was the whole night.
And then, we were marching.
And then my sister, at a certain point she couldn't go anymore-- then she sit.
She sit in the snow.
And imagine with no hair, cold, 3 o'clock in the morning, with no shoes and with no food in your body what suffering it was.
My sister she said, listen.
I can't carry on.
I try for your sake, but I can't anymore.
You go ahead.
If you are alive, tell our brothers.
Because we had two brothers-- we had two brothers in Africa.
We had two brothers in Africa.
That's the one who took us out, you know, from Italy after the concentration.
You know, they were in-- You go ahead, my sister say.
And if you are alive, tell our brothers how we-- how we die.
How we loved them and everything.
And say thank you for all the help they've given us while we were in Rhodes Island.
They were sending, you know, money and parcels and all this.
But that is the end.
I can't do nothing.
And then, what I did, I sat next to my sister.
I said to her, because I give her courage.
Listen, if you sit it.
I'm not standing either.
If you come out, I'll stand myself.
Otherwise, I will kill-- I will die with you.
And then, and then my sister, you know, she stand up for my sake.
After a few kilometers, it was the other way around.
But it was one another giving us courage.
And then we had-- we arrived.
And then we were walking and walking and walking during the night.
Eventually, you know, it was-- all the time, the Germans on all the sides.
We were surrounded by Germans with guns in their hands.
Eventually, comes, like you say a second time, a motorbike.
Now, you tell me if he's not God, this one, what it is.
Because what happened to us twice in the same period.
Come a motorbike and tell the Germans that they were, you know, with us.
If they want to be alive, to-- to run for their lives.
The place where we're supposed to be taken to be-- you know, to be killed has already been occupied by the Germans-- by the Russians.
They better run if they don't want to be killed, themselves.
So we didn't know what they spoke.
We didn't know a thing.
We were just waiting because now, and every two hours or so, they will tell us stop.
We will stop five seconds or five minutes, and we'd carry on.
But now, they say stop, while they were talking.
And we still-- we were stopping.
And waiting and waiting and waiting.
Come 4 o'clock, come 5 o'clock, and then it was dawn and we can see around.
They would look around.
We see not a single German around us.
They all ran in all direction and they left us in the queue.
So when it was-- we're about 200 people.

When it was, you know, dawn-- and these other prisoners, they were all Polish, and all these, they knew all the land and their surroundings because Auschwitz is, you know, in the Polish border, you know, with Russia, whatever.
And then, they knew their land.
We didn't know a thing.

So they all run and they left us standing-- us, we're speaking Spanish-- myself, my sister, another girl from Rhodes Island, two French girls, one Greek girl-- Salonika-- and one man that he was limping.
He was also an old man that we knew.
He was not limping when he arrived at the camp.
He was nice and strong.
But at the time there, he was limping.
The rest-- they abandoned us in the snow.
So we look over all direction.
There we no Germans.
What we can do?
If the Germans came back, they would have found us there standing like little lambs to take it away again.
And then, we look where to go.
We saw in a place, there was a little hut.
We went towards that little hut.
And we went there.
It was empty.
There was nobody there.
But we saw a fire going on.
It must have been the-- the-- the guard hut to keep-- after we saw it was a big, big deposit of coal, where he was guarding.
He was a German guard, I suppose.
He left when the other one left as well.
And you saw in an angle a little fire going on.
And a pail, a pail of water.
You know, there it was there by the-- by the fire.
So it was so nice and warm.
We stayed there.
And then we looked.
There was no Germans to be seen.
We took the pail of water.
We went into the snow, we collected the snow, and we put the pail of water into the fire.
And the snow was melting-- and melting and melting.
And then we had a sip of water.
The first sip of water in a little while.
You will think with all this noise, plenty water.
That is not so.
To melt, you know, and to have just a sip enough for these eight people.
The whole day-- and afterwards-- the whole day the pail has to-- we have to put snow and snow and snow.
When it dissolved, there's enough water for a little bit to each of our eight people.
You see?
So now, that night we had no matches.
And then we say to each other, somebody has to be in charge of the fire.
So we took in turns.
You sleep for a little while.
The other one will wake up and they'll go to sleep.
Not to let-- not to let the fire extinguish.
Because if the fire extinguish, it's the end of us.
There were no matches.
What we can do?
So the whole night that night, we start putting water in the snow and drinking water.
So the next day, we saw around there were no Germans.
One of the other friends, that she was with us, that she was speaking Spanish.
And her name is Stella [NON-ENGLISH].
And after that, she marry very well.
And she was leaving in Nice.
But now she die all the same from natural causes.
So we were assigned to go around in the-- you know-- in the neighborhood to-- to something to eat.
But the Germans have stayed there previously in those big buildings, surely we find something.
Eventually, we find some pieces of bread, some tins of sardines.
But it was so heavy, we had no strength to take it.
If it was mild, we could walk in the whole day we were gone to find food for the other people staying in our hut.
What we did, we find a piece of plank.
And we find a piece of coal.
We tie it up and we make like a sledge to bring to the place where the other people were staying.
And like that, we know-- we were-- we were eating and drinking water and keeping the thing alive.
We had plenty of coal.
We find not just into the hut-- was a big deposit of coal.
And we were just keeping alive, you know, the fire day and night and eating whatever we find.
If I tell you the things we saw in those apartments from the Germans, unbelievable.
There was plenty of Persian carpet, maybe they have taken from the Jewish.
You see jewelry.
We see anything.
But we were not too interested.
What we are looking is food.
Whatever find a piece of sardine, and piece this and piece that, we were taking that to the barracks.
And then after, you know-- 15-- like you say, one week of this thing, my sister say, you know, that you go every day to find things in the here and there.
Can't you find me a piece of fish?
I find like a-- at first she felt like having a piece of fish.
And then, you know, I said, where can I find fish?
You are in Poland.
There is no sea in that.
Like you say like a miracle, no miracle.
The next day I went hunting with my friend to look for food in the apartment, I saw in the middle of the snow a tiny fish lying there in the snow.
I took the fish.
I took it to my sister and she ate it alive, as it was, you know, frozen and everything.
